profile.title: About
profile.html:
Ángel A. Morán Ledezma was born and raised in Ecatepec, Estado de México. He is currently
completing his PhD at the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT), in the Department of
Civil Engineering, Geo and Environmental Sciences. His Master's degree in pure mathematics
— specialising in algebraic geometry — was obtained at CINVESTAV, Mexico City.

The shape of a tree

His mathematical work is organised around a single guiding idea he calls the shape of a tree.
A tree — whether as an object in graph theory or as the topological representation of an
ultrametric space — encodes the geometry of a surprising number of natural phenomena.

In biology, the evolutionary history of a species is represented by a tree: time flows
from the root, and branching events mark the divergence of lineages. In physics, tree-like
hierarchical structures capture the energy landscape underlying the dynamics of proteins and
glasses. In network science — the mathematics of pairwise relationships, from social
interactions to disease spreading to brain connectivity — a ubiquitous phenomenon known as
community structure organises nodes into nested hierarchies: communities within communities,
all the way down. Finding those communities is equivalent to recovering the tree that encodes
the hierarchy. River networks offer yet another natural instance of the same branching geometry.

The tools he brings to these questions are drawn from the theory of stochastic processes
(continuous-time Markov chains), heat kernels, and spectral geometry — a framework for
understanding geometric objects through the spectrum of an associated operator, and one he
finds endlessly fertile.

Art and science

Like many thinkers before him, the bridge between art and science has been a lifelong
fascination. Understanding human experience through psychology and art is, for him, the
richest language available for expressing what he does as a photographer and writer.
